title: Epitaph On The Tomb Of Sir Edward Giles And His Wife In The South Aisle Of Dean Prior Church, Devon.
content:
No trust to metals nor to marbles, when
    These have their fate and wear away as men;
    Times, titles, trophies may be lost and spent,
    But virtue rears the eternal monument.
    What more than these can tombs or tombstones pay?
    But here's the sunset of a tedious day:
    These two asleep are: I'll but be undress'd
    And so to bed: pray wish us all good rest.
meaning:
"Epitaph On The Tomb Of Sir Edward Giles And His Wife In The South Aisle Of Dean Prior Church, Devon." is a quintessential example of Robert Herrick's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
